executiveSummary: Spinout Intelligence Graph helps universities, accelerators, and seed investors discover promising spinouts earlier by unifying company, people, grants, patents, and traction signals into one trusted graph. The buyer is typically a head of ventures or scouting who needs faster ecosystem mapping, better entity resolution, and a repeatable diligence workflow. The wedge is one region or university system with high-quality source connectors, then expansion into alerts, relationship maps, and broader commercialization intelligence. The opportunity is attractive because institutional demand for startup intelligence is rising, but the main risks are data fragmentation, long sales cycles, and the need to maintain graph trust.

market.summary: Startup graph and diligence software for universities, accelerators, and seed investors that need to discover spinouts earlier, resolve messy entity data, and track commercialization signals in one place.
market.whyNow: University ecosystem teams are buying better startup intelligence, and spinout commercialization is getting more structured support and funding.
market.wedge: Start with one region or university system, then expand into alerts, entity resolution, relationship maps, and diligence workspaces.
market.buyingProcess: A pilot usually starts with one ecosystem or portfolio slice, then expands after the buyer sees cleaner entity resolution, faster diligence, and a more complete map of spinouts and relationships.

product.mvp: Ingest a narrow set of sources, normalize companies and people, resolve entities, and provide a searchable graph with alerts and a basic diligence workspace.
product.sixMonth: Add more connectors, richer relationship maps, source provenance, watchlists, and workflow tools for ecosystem scouting and memo prep.
product.twelveMonth: Expand into portfolio monitoring, grant and patent linkage, collaboration history, and shared workspaces for institutional teams.
product.twentyFourMonth: Evolve into a commercialization intelligence platform with automated opportunity scoring, multi-ecosystem benchmarking, and partner data sharing.
keyBets: Entity resolution and source provenance will be the trust layer.
The first buyer values ecosystem completeness over generic startup coverage.
Alerts and diligence workflows create recurring usage beyond one-off searches.

gtm.wedge: Start with one university system or regional ecosystem where fragmented data creates obvious pain.
channels: Partnerships with data vendors and ecosystem operators
gtm.funnelTargets: Target 20 discovery conversations, 8 technical evaluations, 4 paid pilots, and 2 production launches in the first 9 months.
gtm.pricing: Annual subscription plus data enrichment fees; early pilots can be fixed-fee engagements tied to ecosystem coverage and diligence outcomes.

ask.useOfFundsSummary: Fund core engineering, source connector development, entity resolution, 3-5 design-partner deployments, and founder-led go-to-market.

verdict.whyBelieve: The buyer is identifiable, the pain is recurring, and better coverage plus trustable graph quality can create strong workflow lock-in.
verdict.whyDoubt: Data fragmentation, long institutional sales cycles, and competitor breadth could slow adoption and compress differentiation.
verdict.nextDiligence: Interview 10 innovation leaders and investors, inspect 3 live ecosystem datasets, and test whether the graph consistently shortens discovery and diligence time.

firstCustomer.title: Head of ventures at a university innovation office or seed fund
firstCustomer.profile: Needs a reliable map of spinouts, people, and signals across a defined ecosystem and currently relies on spreadsheets and manual research.
firstCustomer.trigger: A missed spinout, a new commercialization mandate, or a diligence bottleneck forces the team to replace ad hoc research.
firstCustomer.initialContract: Paid pilot for one ecosystem with success criteria on coverage, alert quality, and time saved on diligence.

mustBeTrue: At least 3 of the first 5 pilots must show materially better ecosystem coverage than the buyer's current workflow.
Median time to produce a diligence memo must fall by at least 50% on scoped use cases.
At least 70% of pilot customers must continue using the graph after the initial pilot period.
Source coverage and entity resolution quality must stay high enough to preserve buyer trust.
No incumbent data platform should fully own the spinout-discovery wedge in the target segment.
diligenceQuestions: How incomplete is the current ecosystem map, and what coverage threshold makes the product indispensable?
Which sources most reliably predict high-value spinouts before they become obvious?
What workflow improvement is most valuable: discovery, alerting, or diligence prep?
How much manual cleanup is acceptable before graph trust breaks down?
Can the company build durable proprietary coverage faster than broad data incumbents can copy it?

riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Entity resolution errors remain common across pilot datasets
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Start with narrow ecosystems and prioritize source quality over breadth.

riskHeatmap.risk: Institutional sales cycles are too long
riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Pilots do not convert to annual contracts within one budget cycle
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Lead with urgent diligence workflows and small paid pilots.

riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Prospects view the product as interchangeable with broad data vendors
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Differentiate on ecosystem-specific coverage, provenance, and workflow depth.
